items.question: What is training vs inference in supervised ML?
items.answer: Training learns parameters θ from labeled (x, y) by minimizing loss and updating θ. Inference applies fixed θ to new x via ŷ = f_θ(x) without labels or parameter updates.

items.question: What does fit(X, y) do compared to predict(X)?
items.answer: fit(X, y) is training: learn model parameters from labeled pairs. predict(X) is inference: return predictions ŷ for feature rows in X without needing y.

items.question: Why is backward pass not used during inference?
items.answer: Inference has no label y to define a training loss, and θ must stay frozen for stable production behavior. Backward computes gradients for updating θ — only needed during training.

items.question: How do you evaluate a model without retraining on validation data?
items.answer: After fit on train data, run predict(X_val) to get ŷ, then compare to known y_val with metrics. Do not call fit again on validation rows — evaluation uses inference plus labels only for scoring.

items.question: What is training–serving skew?
items.answer: When features or preprocessing at inference differ from those used during fit(), so the model receives a different input distribution than it was trained on, causing silent prediction errors in production.

items.question: Why use model.eval() and torch.no_grad() in PyTorch inference?
items.answer: eval() disables training-specific layer behavior (e.g. dropout); no_grad() skips gradient computation and reduces memory — both keep inference fast and prevent accidental θ updates.
